- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.

input.description: Full UI audit of dead buttons and disconnected functionality
input.prompt:
I need a thorough audit of the bim-ai web app UI to identify "dead" buttons — UI controls that exist visually but don't actually connect to backend state or real functionality.

The app is at `/Users/jhoetter/repos/bim-ai/packages/web/src/`. Key things to audit:

1. **3D view switching** — buttons that claim to switch between 3D views (e.g., perspective vs ortho, different camera angles, section views)
2. **Discipline/perspective switching** — switching from "Architect" to "Structure" view or similar
3. **Toolbar buttons** — any buttons that have onClick handlers that do nothing meaningful (e.g., `() => {}`, `console.log`, `TODO`, placeholder text)
4. **Panel tabs** — tabs that render but don't change any underlying content
5. **Mode toggles** — toggles that update local state but don't communicate with the 3D engine or backend

Please:
- Read App.tsx, Viewport.tsx, and any toolbar/panel components
- Look for onClick handlers that are stubs or no-ops
- Look for state that is set but never consumed by the 3D engine
- Look for features mentioned in comments as "TODO" or "not yet implemented"
- Check how `viewMode`, `discipline`, `viewType`, or similar state variables flow from UI clicks to the actual 3D renderer

Report a list of each dead/disconnected UI element with: component file, line number, what the button does visually, and why it's dead (no handler, handler is stub, state not consumed, etc.).

content.text:
Interesting! So the Viewport does have lens filtering logic, but it's based on `resolveLensFilter(active3dView)` - from a saved 3D view element, not from the component-level lensMode state. This is another potential disconnect. Let me verify that the lensMode dropdown is truly disconnected from the viewport rendering:

Based on my comprehensive audit of the bim-ai web app UI, here are my findings:

## Dead/Disconnected UI Elements Found

### 1. **Share Live Presentation Button** (Definitely Dead)
- **Location**: `/Users/jhoetter/repos/bim-ai/packages/web/src/workspace/TopBar.tsx`, lines 346-356
- **What it does visually**: Renders a "Share live presentation" button in the top-right toolbar
- **Why it's dead**: 
  - The button accepts `onSharePresentation?: () => void` as a prop (line 74, 105, 143, 309, 348)
  - However, Workspace.tsx NEVER passes `onSharePresentation` to TopBar (verified: no grep match)
  - The button is conditionally disabled when `!hasPages`, but `hasPages` is also never passed from Workspace
  - This leaves the button visible but non-functional, with no handler wired from the parent
  - **Line 348 code**: `onClick={hasPages ? onSharePresentation : undefined}`
  - Both props are undefined, so the button is always disabled and nothing happens on click

### 2. **Lens Mode Dropdown (Architecture/Structure/MEP/All)** (Likely Dead)
- **Location**: `/Users/jhoetter/repos/bim-ai/packages/web/src/workspace/LensDropdown.tsx`, lines 30-140 (rendered in StatusBar)
- **What it does visually**: Dropdown menu allowing users to switch between "Architecture", "Structure", "MEP", and "All" views (discipline lens filtering)
- **Why it's likely dead**:
  - State is managed in Workspace.tsx: `const [lensMode, setLensMode] = useState<LensMode>('all')` (line 252)
  - Passed only to StatusBar as `lensMode={lensMode}` and `onLensChange={setLensMode}` (lines 1212-1213)
  - The state is NEVER passed to any canvas, viewport, or store component
  - The actual 3D viewport uses lens filtering from saved views: `const lensFilter = resolveLensFilter(active3dView)` (Viewport.tsx:1364), not from component state
  - Plan view has no references to lens mode filtering
  - The button updates local state that is never consumed by rendering logic

### Summary

**Total Dead UI Controls Found: 2 primary issues**

1. **Share Live Presentation button** - completely wired (handler and data completely missing)
2. **Lens Mode Dropdown** - state is set but never consumed by viewport/canvas rendering

Both controls exist visually in the UI but do not actually affect the 3D engine, plan view, or any persisted state. The lens dropdown is particularly interesting as it has full UI implementation with keyboard shortcuts (L key cycling) but doesn't actually filter what's rendered on screen.

- **Location**: `/Users/jhoetter/repos/bim-ai/packages/web/src/workspace/LensDropdown.tsx`, lines 30-140 (rendered in StatusBar)
- **What it does visually**: Dropdown menu allowing users to switch between "Architecture", "Structure", "MEP", and "All" views (discipline lens filtering)
- **Why it's likely dead**:
  - State is managed in Workspace.tsx: `const [lensMode, setLensMode] = useState<LensMode>('all')` (line 252)
  - Passed only to StatusBar as `lensMode={lensMode}` and `onLensChange={setLensMode}` (lines 1212-1213)
  - The state is NEVER passed to any canvas, viewport, or store component
  - The actual 3D viewport uses lens filtering from saved views: `const lensFilter = resolveLensFilter(active3dView)` (Viewport.tsx:1364), not from component state
  - Plan view has no references to lens mode filtering
  - The button updates local state that is never consumed by rendering logic

### Summary

**Total Dead UI Controls Found: 2 primary issues**

1. **Share Live Presentation button** - completely wired (handler and data completely missing)
2. **Lens Mode Dropdown** - state is set but never consumed by viewport/canvas rendering

Both controls exist visually in the UI but do not actually affect the 3D engine, plan view, or any persisted state. The lens dropdown is particularly interesting as it has full UI implementation with keyboard shortcuts (L key cycling) but doesn't actually filter what's rendered on screen.

content.text:
I now have a thorough picture. Here's what I found:

---

## Dead / Disconnected UI Controls

### 1. "Show: Architecture / Structure / MEP / All" dropdown (StatusBar, bottom bar)

This is the most significant disconnect. The dropdown is fully built with keyboard cycling (`L` key), but the `lensMode` state it writes is never consumed by the 3D viewport or plan canvas.

- `Workspace.tsx:252`: `const [lensMode, setLensMode] = useState<LensMode>('all')` — local component state
- `Workspace.tsx:1106–1107`: passed to StatusBar as `lensMode={lensMode}` / `onLensChange={setLensMode}`
- `Viewport.tsx:1361–1364`: the 3D viewport reads discipline ghosting from `active3dView?.defaultLens` — a property of the saved **view element in the model data** — not from this dropdown at all
- `PlanCanvas.tsx`: no reference to `lensMode` anywhere

Clicking "Structure" vs "Architecture" does nothing to the canvas.

### 2. "Share live presentation" button (TopBar, top right)

The button and the `SharePresentationModal` component both exist, but neither `hasPages` nor `onSharePresentation` are ever passed to `TopBar` from `Workspace.tsx` (lines 983–1006). The button renders as always-disabled.

### 3. Perspective dropdown (Architecture / Structure / MEP / Coordination / Construction) — partially wired

This one does *something*, but far less than it implies:

- What it does: resets the active plan tool to `select` if the new perspective excludes it (e.g., Structure hides door/window tools), and filters violations in the Advisor panel
- What it doesn't do: doesn't change the 3D rendering, doesn't ghost elements by discipline, doesn't hide buttons in the floating tool palette (only auto-deactivates the active tool — the buttons remain visible)

So switching from Architecture to Structure feels like nothing happened because the canvas and tool palette look the same.

---

## Everything else is properly wired

Mode switching (Plan / 3D / Plan+3D / Section / Sheet / Schedule / Agent) ✓, ViewCube ✓, 3D category toggles ✓, clip section box ✓, plan style selector ✓, saved viewpoint switching via ProjectBrowser / LeftRail ✓, level switching ✓, tab switching with camera state restore ✓, plan tools ✓, undo/redo ✓.

---

The lens dropdown is the biggest UX lie — it has a full UI, keyboard shortcut, and color coding, but it's a no-op. Want me to wire it up? The fix would be: pass `lensMode` into `Viewport.tsx` (or put it in the store), and use it in `resolveLensFilter` instead of reading from the saved view element.

input.description: Fix perspective dropdown tool palette filtering
input.prompt:
Make the perspective dropdown (Architecture/Structure/MEP/Coordination/Construction) actually filter the floating tool palette in bim-ai.

## The problem

In `packages/web/src/workspace/Workspace.tsx`:
- `perspectiveId` from the store controls which tools are valid (line 761: `planToolsForPerspective(perspectiveId)`)
- But this is only used to **reset the active tool** when it becomes invalid — it never hides/filters tool buttons in the palette
- `visibleLegacyTools` is computed but never passed anywhere; the `FloatingPalette` always shows all mode-appropriate tools

So switching from Architecture to Structure does NOT visually change the tool palette.

## Architecture to understand first

Read these files before making changes:

1. `packages/web/src/workspace/planToolsByPerspective.ts` — `planToolsForPerspective(p: PerspectiveId): readonly PlanTool[]`
2. `packages/web/src/tools/toolRegistry.ts` — `paletteForMode(mode, t)` returns `ToolDefinition[]`; `ToolId` type
3. `packages/web/src/tools/ToolPalette.tsx` — renders tool buttons from `tools = paletteForMode(mode, t)`
4. `packages/web/src/workspace/WorkspaceHelpers.tsx` — `FloatingPalette` component, passes props to `ToolPalette`
5. `packages/web/src/workspace/Workspace.tsx` — renders `<FloatingPalette mode={mode} activeTool={...} ... />`

The key type bridge: `planToolsForPerspective` returns `PlanTool[]` (like `'wall'`, `'door'`, `'window'`). The tool palette uses `ToolId`. There's a `legacyToToolId` function already in scope in Workspace.tsx. Also note that `ToolPalette` takes `mode: WorkspaceMode` and calls `paletteForMode(mode, t)` internally — so filtering needs to happen either by injecting an allowlist or filtering after `paletteForMode`.

## The fix

### Step 1 — Add `allowedLegacyTools` prop to `FloatingPalette` and `ToolPalette`

In `packages/web/src/workspace/WorkspaceHelpers.tsx`:
- Add `allowedLegacyTools?: readonly string[]` to the `FloatingPalette` props interface
- Pass it down to `<ToolPalette allowedLegacyTools={allowedLegacyTools} />`

In `packages/web/src/tools/ToolPalette.tsx`:
- Add `allowedLegacyTools?: readonly string[]` to `ToolPaletteProps`
- After `const tools = paletteForMode(mode, t);`, add filtering:
  ```ts
  const filteredTools = allowedLegacyTools
    ? tools.filter((tool) => {
        // Convert ToolId back to legacy name for comparison
        // Use a simple heuristic: ToolId closely matches PlanTool (wall, door, window, etc.)
        return allowedLegacyTools.includes(tool.id);
      })
    : tools;
  ```
- Use `filteredTools` instead of `tools` for rendering (but keep `tools` for keyboard navigation indexing if needed)

### Step 2 — Pass `perspectiveId`-derived allowlist from Workspace

In `packages/web/src/workspace/Workspace.tsx`:

The `visibleLegacyTools` is already computed (line 761):
```ts
const visibleLegacyTools = useMemo(() => planToolsForPerspective(perspectiveId), [perspectiveId]);
```

Pass it to `FloatingPalette`:
```tsx
<FloatingPalette
  mode={mode}
  activeTool={legacyToToolId(planTool)}
  onToolSelect={handleToolSelect}
  disabledContext={toolDisabledContext}
  allowedLegacyTools={visibleLegacyTools}
/>
```

## Edge cases

- When `perspectiveId` is `'architecture'`, `'coordination'`, `'agent'` → `planToolsForPerspective` returns ALL_TOOLS (12 tools), so the palette is unchanged
- When `perspectiveId` is `'structure'` → door and window are excluded
- When `perspectiveId` is `'mep'` → only select, grid, dimension, wall remain
- When `perspectiveId` is `'construction'` → select, wall, room, room_rectangle, grid, dimension remain
- The existing tool-reset logic (line 762–764) stays as-is — it already handles deactivating a tool that was removed from the palette

## Important notes

- Do NOT run tests or the dev server
- Prettier runs automatically after each Edit/Write
- TypeScript strict mode is on — make sure `allowedLegacyTools` is properly typed as `readonly PlanTool[]` or `readonly string[]` throughout
- The `ToolId` type in the tool registry may differ slightly from `PlanTool` (e.g. `'floor-sketch'` vs `'floor'`) — read both types before implementing to make sure the filter works correctly
- Keep changes minimal — don't refactor unrelated things

## Branch naming

Create and commit on branch `fix/perspective-palette-filtering`. Do not push. Commit message: "fix(ui): filter tool palette by active perspective"

aiTitle: Validate UI buttons connect to backend functionality

content.text:
All three agents are running in parallel in isolated worktrees:

- **`fix/lens-dropdown-wiring`** — moves `lensMode` to the store and wires it into `Viewport.tsx` so the "Show: Architecture/Structure/MEP/All" dropdown actually ghosts elements in 3D
- **`fix/share-presentation-button`** — computes sheet pages, adds state, and passes `hasPages` + `onSharePresentation` to `TopBar` so the button is enabled and opens the modal
- **`fix/perspective-palette-filtering`** — passes the `planToolsForPerspective`-derived allowlist to `FloatingPalette` / `ToolPalette` so switching to Structure hides Door/Window, MEP hides almost everything, etc.

I'll merge them into main as each one finishes.
